data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Walmart Sinks On Cautious Outlook
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify.

Stock falls with guidance below expectations. (0:15) OpenAI hits 400 million weekly users. (3:41) Amazon will deliver the next Bond film. (4:11)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far. Walmart’s (NYSE:WMT) future guidance is spooking investors and shares are sharply lower.

While Q4 results topped analysts’ expectations, the outlook assumes a generally stable consumer and continued pressure from its mix of products and formats globally.

The expectation for Q1 sales to be up a 3% to 4% should result in EPS between $0.57 and $0.58. That’s down 3 to 4 cents from a year ago and below the consensus estimate of $0.65. For FY26, Walmart also expects sales to improve 3%-4%, translating to $698.1 billion at the midpoint with EPS between $2.50 to $2.60 per share. This is also less than Wall Street’s expectations for EPS of $2.76 per share on $705.14 billion in sales.

However, shares did come off their lows, during the earnings call, where CEO Doug McMillan said “once you take the noise out of guidance, we’re in a great spot to start the year as we grow profits faster than sales.”

The sell side also offered support. Jefferies analyst Corey Tarlowe said: “We think WMT is setting up '25 to be a year of beats & raises (similar to '24).”

Morgan Stanley’s Simeon Gutman said: “Street estimates are likely to be revised down this morning. Similar to FY25, we would expect a path of outperformance in FY26 and ultimate EPS power exceeding the initial outlook.”

Walmart also raised its dividend by 13% to $0.235 per share.

Among other active stocks, Palantir’s (PLTR) dive in the previous session, on potential defense spending cuts, continues today as well as the stock is sinking again. Shares are currently down 20% from Tuesday’s close.

However, Wedbush analyst Dan Ives came to the company’s defense, saying: “The bears, which have hated Palantir from $12 to $120 in the last 18 months, now have found their latest 'silver bullet' negative thesis around PLTR being exposed to these budget cuts.”

“This is exactly the opposite (of) how we believe these DOD cuts will play out as in our view Palantir's unique software approach will enable the company to gain more IT budget dollars at the Pentagon....not less.”

Alibaba (BABA) is sharply up after topping quarterly consensus numbers. Key takeaways included the beats for revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and cloud intelligence segment sales.

Morgan Stanley said the earnings report strengthened its investment thesis as it made modest upward revisions to its EPS estimates following the print. Meanwhile, Jefferies highlighted that the Alibaba's International Digital Commerce Group revenue rose 32% year-over-year to smash expectations.

On the economic front, initial jobless claims rose by 5,000 to 219,000, a little more than expected.

The federal government's campaign to cut costs and fire workers doesn't appear to be affecting DC-area claims so far. About 20% of federal workers live in the metro area. Initial claims filed in the District of Columbia rose by 13 to 1,695; claims filed in Maryland dropped by 821 to 2,354, and claims filed in Virginia declined by 397 to 2,805.

Samuel Tombs, chief U.S. economist at Pantheon Macro, says: "any laid off federal employees likely will file for claims in the coming weeks, particularly if the layoffs broaden out, as seems likely. Next week’s numbers likely will also receive an uplift from extreme winter weather in parts of the country."

In other news of note, OpenAI had 400 million weekly active users for ChatGPT as of February. It’s a 33% increase from the 300 million weekly active users as of Dec. 4, 2024. The company had more than 2 million paying business users, compared to 1 million as of Sept. 5.

Apple's (AAPL) integration of OpenAI's AI model started in December last year. Apple Intelligence relies on a mix of Apple’s own AI technology and a collaboration with OpenAI.

Amazon MGM Studios (AMZN) is taking over the creative development of the James Bond franchise, with longtime producers Barbara Broccoli and Michael G. Wilson stepping back. Amazon MGM, Wilson and Broccoli have formed a new joint venture to house the intellectual property rights will remain co-owners of 007. Financial terms were not disclosed.

Amazon gained distribution rights to all the James Bond movies after it bought Metro Goldwyn Mayer for $8.5 billion back in 2022. With the current announcement, the company will also be responsible for creative control and production of future Bond films.

Possible upcoming Amazon-Bond titles already in development: Dr. No Returns, From Russia With Free Shipping, You Only Click Twice, On Her Majesty's Same-Day Service and Live and Let Prime.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Goldman chief economist Jan Hatzius says changes in AI-related capital expenditures could see just limited near-term implications, despite the DeepSeek scares.

He says competition could “catalyze higher levels of real hardware spending if it pushes incumbents to invest more to maintain their lead.” Goldman Sachs analysts have, in fact, increased their estimates of 2025 AI-related investment on net over the last quarter.

Capex expectations for 2025 were revised up by 5% for S&P 1500 (SPTM) companies over the last quarter but were only revised up by 2% for companies with broad exposure to tariffs and revised down by 1% for companies with a high reported share of sales to Canada, Mexico, and China.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: I'm Buying Discounted Dividend Icons Up To 6% Yield
data.description:
Companies that can be counted upon to deliver shareholder returns year in and out tend to have moat-worthy attributes that stem from scale and barriers to entry. Examples of industries with such traits include pharmaceutical companies with built-up scale and expertise, as well as patent protection for their drugs. Other examples include consumer staples like Coca-Cola (KO) with premium brand recognition and REITs that have achieved a scale that results in operating efficiencies and low cost of debt.

This brings me to the following 2 names, both of which carry the aforementioned traits. Better yet, both of these stocks are trading at attractive valuations, supporting yields up to 6%, which is 5x more than that of the S&P 500 (SPY).

I consider both stocks to be dividend icons due to their long operating history and sizable asset and knowledge base that’s been built up over multiple decades. In this article, I explore what makes each a solid buy for value and income investors, so let’s dive in!

#1: Merck (MRK)

Merck stock has had a hard time catching a bid as of late as challenges around some of its key franchises have made the market extra cautious around the company. This has resulted in a staggering 34% drop in share price over the trailing 12 months. At the current price of $82.88, MRK trades at a forward PE of just 9.2 and is closing in on a 4% dividend yield, as shown below.

Contrary to what MRK’s share price performance might suggest, it actually produced solid 2024 results, during which MRK saw 10% revenue growth on an FX-neutral basis. This includes solid performance in Q4, during which total revenue grew by 9% YoY on an FX-neutral basis.

This was driven by a continued solid showing from Keytruda, which saw 21% YoY sales growth to $7.8 billion in Q4 alone due to increased uptake in metastatic and earlier-stage cancers. Animal Health also saw strong 13% YoY sales growth during the quarter, reflecting strong demand for livestock and companion animal products such as Bravecto and Scalibor for treatment and prevention of parasites.

Moreover, MRK launched Winrevair, which is a novel treatment for pulmonary arterial hypertension, which contributed $200 million in sales with strong patient uptake, and excluding China, Gardasil (an HPV vaccine) sales grew by double-digits due to robust demand in international markets.

Despite the strong results, the market appears to be concerned around a challenging environment for Gardasil in China. The drug has come under pressure there due to lower discretionary spending and slow recovery in demand. This has resulted in overall channel inventory levels remaining elevated and management has responded by temporarily pausing shipments to China through at least the middle of this year.

Looking ahead, market concerns around Merck and Gardasil in particular may be overblown, as business remains healthy in international markets and management still sees significant long-term opportunity for Gardasil in China due to the high population that are not yet immunized. Merck also has a strong pipeline of 20 drugs to counter the eventual loss of exclusivity of Keytruda. This includes innovative new drugs to treat RSV in infants and combination drugs for HIV. Management is guiding for adjusted EPS of $8.96 at the midpoint of range, which represents a robust 17% growth rate from $7.65 achieved in 2024.

MRK maintains a strong A+ credit rating with stable outlook from S&P. This is supported by a strong balance sheet with a very low net debt to TTM EBITDA of just 0.87x. Recent share price weakness has pushed the dividend yield up to 3.9%. The dividend is well-protected by a 40% payout ratio and comes with a 5-year CAGR of 7.7% and 14 consecutive years of growth.

MRK is solidly in value range at the current price of $83.29 with a forward PE of just 9.26x, sitting far below its normal PE of 13.96, as shown below.

With a market overreaction on Gardasil, which is temporary, in my opinion, a 3.9% dividend yield and analyst estimates for EPS growth of 10% annually over the next two years, MRK could well deliver potential market-beating total returns from here. Plus, any reversion to the mean valuation could be an added bonus for value investors.

#2: W. P. Carey (WPC)

W. P. Carey strikes me as another dividend stock that’s trading in value range and is hard for investors to go wrong with over the long run. WPC stock has recently caught a bid on strong Q4 results, but still trades at a low P/FFO of 12x with a 6% dividend yield, as shown below.

At present, WPC carries a portfolio of 1,555 properties that are diversified across 355 tenants. It has a long weighted average lease term of 12.3 years and 98.6% occupancy. As shown below, WPC’s properties are mostly industrial and warehouse at a combined 63% of ABR. Retail makes up 22% and much of the rest comes from self-storage.

WPC took steps to de-risk its portfolio by spinning off its office properties into Net Lease Office Properties (NLOP) in 2023. What remains is a portfolio of mission-critical net lease properties that are leased to credit-worthy tenants. What makes WPC standout is its international exposure, in which it is years ahead of Realty Income Corp. (O). This enables WPC to secure lower interest rates through issuance of Eurobonds compared to what it can get in the U.S. 33% of WPC’s annual base rent comes from Europe while the remainder comes from the U.S.

WPC demonstrated strong Q4 2024 results, which includes record Q4 investment volume of $840 million, bringing full year investment volume to $1.6 billion. AFFO per share grew by 1.7% YoY to $1.21. It’s worth noting that the YoY comparison on AFFO was partially impacted by the NLOP spin-off in Q4 of 2023. Had it not been for the spin-off, AFFO/share would have seen a higher growth rate.

WPC achieved healthy initial investment yields of 7.5% on average during Q4 and over 9% for the full year 2024. This compares favorably to WPC’s cost of debt, which averages below 5%, based on Eurobonds with average pricing in the high 3% range paired with U.S. bonds in the mid-5% range.

Looking ahead, management is guiding for 3% annual AFFO/share growth. This is supported by annual rent bumps in the mid-2% range, with some recent deals reaching the 3% range. External growth is also a driver as WPC aims to dispose non-core properties this year to fund acquisitions such as self-storage and retail properties. Management expects to achieve investment spreads of around 100 bps from this asset recycling, based on mid-6% cap rates on properties sold and mid-7% initial yields on acquired properties.

WPC carries a strong balance sheet with a weighted average interest rate of just 3.2%. This is supported by a BBB+ credit rating with stable outlook from S&P. WPC has solid debt metrics with a debt-to-gross assets ratio of 42%, sitting below the 50% safe mark. It also has a safe debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 5.5x, sitting below the 6.0x safe mark.

Importantly for income investors, WPC currently yields an appealing 5.8% and the dividend is well-covered by a 73% payout ratio. I also find WPC to be attractive at the current price of $60.39 with a forward P/FFO of 12.3, which sits below its historical PE of 12.8. Despite the recent uptick in price, WPC’s valuation remains well below the 13.3 P/FFO of peer Realty Income, which also has international ambitions of its own. With a near-6% dividend yield and management’s conservative guidance for 3% annual FFO/share growth, WPC could well deliver market-level total returns, all with a far higher yield than that of the market average.

Investor Takeaway

Merck and W. P. Carey are both attractively valued dividend juggernauts that have qualities of durable, moat-worthy businesses. Merck offers value and income investors an attractive opportunity amidst temporary headwinds with its promising portfolio and robust drug pipeline. This is supported by a very strong balance sheet and a well-covered dividend.

Meanwhile, W. P. Carey provides a reliable 6% yield and steady growth prospects through strategic asset recycling and access to low cost of capital. Both stocks trade at attractive valuations relative to historical norms, presenting compelling long-term investment opportunities for value and income-focused investors.

data.title: Here's What To Watch In Gold Right Now (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

Gold has momentarily paused just below $3,000, catching its breath while building momentum for its next move. With energy accumulating, a breakout above $3,000 could be just around the corner.

Gold has been on an impressive run lately, making life easy for us gold investors - a great problem to have! Now, it’s all about monitoring its progress, watching key levels, and being aware of the fundamentals pointing to even higher prices.

As gold climbs, expect more naysayers - likely those who missed the rally - claiming it has risen too far, too fast, and is due for a drop. But that’s just part of a classic bull market, which always climbs a ‘wall of worry.’

Starting with the basics, as I’ve mentioned before, I closely watch $100 increments in COMEX gold futures because they often act as key support and resistance levels. For example, $2,800 marked the peak in late October, and once gold broke above it about a month ago, it signaled a strong bullish breakout.

Tracking these key levels isn’t voodoo or pseudoscience - it has a solid logical basis. These price points hold significant psychological value, with investors closely watching them and placing stop and limit orders around them more than at other random levels.

Additionally, they often align with strike prices in the options market that see the highest activity. The combination of these factors is why these $100 increments frequently serve as important floors and ceilings in gold.

As you can see from the chart below, gold has broken above both $2,800 and $2,900—an encouraging sign. Last week, gold tested the $2,900 support multiple times, including during Friday’s sharp pullback, and each time, it rebounded quickly.

This kind of resilience is a strong indicator of gold’s underlying strength.

Right now, I want to see $2,900 hold as support, with the next key hurdle being gold’s push toward—and eventual breakthrough of—$3,000, a level of major psychological significance and strong resistance.

There’s a high probability this will happen because round numbers like $3,000 tend to act as price magnets, drawing asset prices toward them.

If gold can close convincingly above $3,000, we enter serious 'blue sky' territory, where there’s no significant resistance overhead. That’s when I believe we’ll see a parabolic surge - one far more aggressive than anything we’ve witnessed so far. I’m talking about massive $100 up days as momentum accelerates.

An interesting and encouraging development in gold over the past week is the formation of a potential bull flag pattern - a continuation pattern that signals further gains upon a confirmed breakout.

However, for this to be valid, gold must break out to the upside with strong volume. I take a rather-than-predictive approach in these scenarios, waiting for confirmation before making conclusions.

If this pattern successfully plays out, it would provide the momentum gold needs to slice through $3,000.

After gold’s sharp rally since the start of the year, it became overbought according to the widely followed Relative Strength Index (RSI), a key momentum indicator. While this is typical behavior in bull markets and a sign of strength, it often signals a period of consolidation to ease the overbought condition.

Fortunately, the recent pause over the past week has helped moderate this extreme, which is a positive sign. Gold may need a bit more time to consolidate further and cool off before making its next push toward $3,000+.

Gold miners are gaining momentum, though not as quickly as gold itself. That’s perfectly fine. I remain highly optimistic about them, but it may take gold surpassing $3,000 for sentiment to truly ignite.

It’s also important to recognize that gold’s bull market has been largely fueled by Asian central banks and investors, while Western investors have yet to join in. Instead, they’ve been preoccupied with chasing 'Fartcoin' and Dot-com Bubble 2.0 stocks - assets that are destined to implode.

Most gold mining stocks and ETFs trade on Western exchanges, meaning their lackluster performance so far is simply a reflection of Western investors' disinterest in gold. However, I expect that to change very soon.

The large-cap VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) broke out of a long-term triangle pattern about a year ago - a strong signal that a major bull market is underway. Now, for this rally to gain real momentum, GDX needs to decisively close above the key $42 to $46 resistance zone overhead.

Likewise, the VanEck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) broke out of a long-term triangle pattern in early 2024, signaling the start of a new bull market.

For full confirmation, I’m now watching for a decisive close above the key $50–$60 resistance zone, which would solidify the bullish trend and open the door for much further upside.

One of the many signs that gold’s bull market still has plenty of upside is its historical performance following the first Fed funds rate cut in a rate-cutting cycle.

If gold follows the pattern of the last three cycles, it suggests a climb from $2,940 (at the time of writing) to around $3,380.

Another key reason gold’s bull market is still in its infancy is the massive bubble in the U.S. stock market, which will end in a significant bear market. This downturn will lead to a substantial transfer of capital from stocks into gold.

Notably, the Dow-to-Gold ratio broke below its uptrend line in the spring of 2024, signaling that the rotation of capital from stocks to gold has already begun. This shift will gain momentum as the stock market bubble inevitably bursts.

Gold still has an enormous amount of fuel left to propel it to $5,000, $10,000, or even $15,000 and beyond. One key factor is that the U.S. is currently experiencing the largest stock market bubble in history by several measures - including the total U.S. stock market capitalization-to-GDP ratio.

This metric, often called the 'Buffett Indicator,' has been described by Warren Buffett himself as 'the best single measure of where valuations stand at any given moment.' With the equity market stretched to extreme valuations, gold’s upside potential remains massive.

If the Buffett Indicator isn’t enough to convince you, consider the Nasdaq 100 adjusted for the U.S. M2 money supply.

This metric reveals that we’re in a bubble even worse than the late 1990s dot-com bubble, which ended in disaster. While that’s a terrifying prospect for tech stock investors, it’s fantastic news for precious metals investors.

In summary, gold remains in a strong technical position, and I’m watching for it to break out of its recent bull flag pattern and make a run for $3,000. A decisive close above this level would confirm the breakout and likely propel gold’s bull market into a much more powerful phase.

Beyond the technicals, gold still has plenty of room to run, fueled by the looming burst of the U.S. stock market bubble. When that happens, we can expect a significant capital rotation from equities into gold, silver, and mining stocks.

This is an incredibly exciting space to be in, so buckle up and enjoy the ride!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Palantir Stock: Still Overvalued But Don't Panic Sell
data.description:
Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) stock is selling off, down nearly 20% from all-time highs, as concerns over government budget cuts weigh on the company's growth prospects. While this has the potential to hurt one of Palantir's core markets, the long-term prospects of the company and the stock lie in the private sector, not the public sector. I continue to think that PLTR is overvalued and investors should exercise caution when considering buying in at current levels. However, for those who own the shares as a play on the company's domination of the commercial markets down the line, a reduction in government spending does little to negatively affect that thesis.

In my previous article on PLTR, I discussed the company's impressive revenue growth, the attractive value proposition of its AI platform, and solid management, concluding that the company has a bright future despite the stock's apparent gross overvaluation. I argued that this leaves shares open to a major correction in the event of a market disruption or anything that could derail the seemingly unbreakable upward momentum. That article can be read here.

Well, it was bound to happen eventually. After rising more than 500% over the last 12 months, PLTR has finally run into its first major headwind. The stock has fallen 20% in two days over fears that spending cuts at the Department of Defense will impact a significant portion of the company's revenue base. In terms of raw impact, these fears appear to be well-founded, as more than half of Palantir's sales come from government sources:

For FY2024, about 55% of Palantir's revenue came from its government segment, while in Q4 alone that number was 61%. It's not difficult to see why the market was spooked.

The news in question entailed newly minted Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth announcing that the DoD would be slashing its budget by 8% each year over the next 5 years. There are certain projects that are exempt from the cuts, but this represents a major reduction in the money pool available to contractors like Palantir in the short term.

In response, I've seen bulls on Seeking Alpha and elsewhere claim that this is actually a positive catalyst for Palantir. Dan Ives at Wedbush stated the case as follows:

“This is exactly the opposite [of] how we believe these DOD cuts will play out as in our view Palantir's unique software approach will enable the company to gain more IT budget dollars at the Pentagon....not less, despite these initial knee-jerk reactions from the Street... Palantir is so well positioned for this new disciplined spending environment at the Pentagon, and this will ultimately be a positive growth catalyst as the various programs are scrutinized and as Karp & Co. get a bigger seat at the table in the Beltway.”

I see the driving point here, but I'm not quite convinced of the optimism on display.

First, when budgets get cut, the primary effort goes towards getting rid of programs, not replacing them or using AI to efficiently re-create them. Achieving an 8% reduction in spending will be a difficult task in its own right, and I think it's highly unlikely that the DoD will decide to ramp up AI expenditures while it is trying to trim this fat. Palantir's services are expensive and, though they might make processes more efficient, the department will likely be wary to commit to such services during a cost-cutting regime.

Second, even if the DoD and the government do decide to lean on AI for spending efficiencies, there's no guarantee that Palantir will be the one to pick up those dollars. It is currently the market leader, of course. However, it could take years for the US government to buy into an AI makeover and by then who knows what competitors will come out of the woodwork to fight for the billions of dollars at stake. Does Palantir have a good shot at securing some of that as revenue? Absolutely, but, as we can see from the price action, this uncertainty is having a substantial impact.

Third, the stock is not currently priced for “this will probably ultimately benefit the company long-term” -- it's priced for continuous strong revenue growth for 5 years while maintaining margins, at a minimum. Any disruption to that narrative will have consequences, and this has the potential to throw off PLTR's momentum story.

All that said, I do think the sell-off is overdone for one simple reason: the future potential valuation of Palantir is in the rapid proliferation of AI opportunities in the commercial sector, not the government. So personally, while I think the stock is highly overvalued, most investors who bought in at the lofty valuation did so because they believed that the company would continue to expand its footprint horizontally to companies looking to streamline their operations in the age of AI-enabled data analytics.

In its most recent quarter, commercial revenue grew by 64% YOY and closed $803 million in contracts, up 134% YOY and 170% QoQ. This is the real value generator. The company's platform is scalable, maintains a significant operating leverage, and is a cash-generating machine as Palantir booked $517 million in free cash flow in Q4, a 63% margin. The future of PLTR lies here, not the ebbs and flows of a chaotic Trump administration.

For FY 2025, Palantir expects to earn exceeding $1 billion from its commercial division, up 54% YOY, and likely a low-ball estimate if the continuous revenue and earnings beats of FY2024 are any indication. Of course, this is subject to some risk, as AI spending could be one of the first things companies pare back on in the case of a recession. However, in the current environment, Palantir looks poised to continue increasing its customer base and average contract value.

There's also the risk of major volatility in the short term as the stock's momentum breaks down and perhaps leads shares even lower. I will be keeping a close eye out for an entry point, though it's difficult to say currently where that would be. I would likely be a buyer in the low $70 range.

Investor Takeaway

In that vein, I think PLTR shareholders should hold through this storm and ignore the noise surrounding government cuts. There could be some short-term pain as revenue growth takes a hit from reduced spending or loss of contracts, but I think investors should keep the commercial sector in focus.

As for valuation, PLTR remains highly speculative as the stock trades at around 175 times FY2025 earnings estimates, and I will not be buying in anytime soon. But for those that believe in the long-term growth narrative and revenue acceleration from capitalizing on the increased AI spending in the private sector, I think the most recent news is no reason to change that thesis. Therefore, I am re-iterating a Hold on PLTR.

Thanks for reading and best of luck!

data.title: Adyen: High Growth, High Quality Business Deserves A Premium Multiple
data.description:
Summary

I am positive on Adyen (OTCPK:ADYEY). My summarized thesis is that I expect ADYEY’s strong growth momentum to persist, supported by increased adoption of its unified commerce and platform solutions. While it is debatable whether ADYEY should trade at a premium multiple, I lean towards having an optimistic view, as I believe the market will continue to value ADYEY at a high multiple if it can continue to grow >20%.

Company Overview

ADYEY is a payments processor company. It provides a platform for processing transactions across various channels such as online, mobile, and in-store. The unique aspect about the ADYEY platform is that it operates on a single, end-to-end infrastructure. What this means is that there are no intermediaries, and this allows merchants to accept payments more efficiently. This unique model has enabled ADYEY to become a partner for major customers like Amazon, eBay, McDonald's, etc.

Earnings Results Update

ADYEY 4Q24 results were nothing but spectacular. Net revenue grew 23% y/y to EUR 584 million, supported by total processed volume (TPV) growth of 15% y/y to EUR346 billion and stable take rates of ~17 bps. Looking deeper into the 15% y/y TPV growth paints a much more positive picture of ADYEY's growth momentum. Unified Commerce TPV grew 36% y/y, as more merchants adopted ADYEY’s in-store payment solutions, driving the segment to a percentage mix of 22% of total TPV, up from 19% in 3Q24. Another way to envision the strength behind this momentum can be seen from the fact that ADYEY added 63,000 new transacting terminals in 4Q24, significantly above the average of 8,000 in previous quarters. Aside from unified commerce, ADYEY’s Platforms business, which includes embedded finance and issuing, also saw solid growth momentum, with TPV growing 44% y/y. The number of platform customers processing >EUR1 billion in TPV grew to 28 from 25 in 3Q24, which I take as a very strong sign that ADYEY is successfully penetrating the large enterprise market. On the profitability front, ADYEY saw EUR569 million in EBITDA for 2H24 (52.6% margin).

Growth Drivers and Catalysts

The two major growth drivers are ADYEY’s Unified Commerce and Platforms business. For Unified Commerce, I expect growth to remain very robust in the coming periods as merchants are prioritizing frictionless customer experiences. The trend is apparent, and one good instance that highlights the importance of this is Chili’s move to enable customers to pay at their tables (helped to improve turnover rates and customer experience). This is right up ADYEY’s alley, given its strength in seamlessly integrating online and offline transactions. While this report may be slightly dated, it gives a sense of the growth potential as a sizeable mix of retailers is still using legacy payment systems (and this article talks about the downside of legacy payment systems). It might take a while for retailers to upgrade their payment systems (because of how labor- and cost-intensive it can be), but I believe it is only a matter of time; if not, these retailers will lose demand (like what happened to Chili's).

As for the Platforms segment, the growth potential is even more substantial. To reiterate, ADYEY saw TPV growth of 44% y/y in 4Q24 and has seen a growing number of platform customers generating large transaction volumes. What makes me excited about this segment is not only because it contributes to volume growth but also because it improves the visibility to growth. For those that do not follow this segment closely, through the platform segment, ADYEY provides solutions like embedded payments, merchant onboarding solutions, virtual and physical payment card solutions, merchant lending solutions, etc., and the nature of such services is highly recurring. The growth runway here is extremely long as well. Using third-party research estimates, this segment is expected to grow at >30% CAGR between 2024 and 2033, and I expect ADYEY to fully capitalize on this.

Valuation

I see ADYEY as a business with a lot of growth momentum in an industry with plenty of room to grow. It has proven its ability to execute well, move into adjacent verticals (the platform segment, for instance), and expand margins (showing that it is growing under good cost discipline). The biggest debate is what multiple ADYEY should trade at. The bears will say that the ADYEY valuation multiple is expensive at ~14x forward revenue, given the premium relative to other payment peers like Fiserv (FI) at 7.4x forward revenue or PayPal Holdings (PYPL) at 2.3x. I disagree. I think ADYEY deserves to trade at a premium multiple because of the quality of its growth. Remember that ADYEY is delivering >20% y/y growth in a tough macro environment, so growth can be much better in a normalized business environment. This contrasts deeply with FI and PYPL, which are growing <10% y/y. I would argue that a better comp for ADYEY is Shopify (SHOP)—a fast-growing e-commerce/payments company (SHOP is expected to grow revenue by >20% over the next two years and trades at 14x forward revenue). My belief is that so long as ADYEY can continue growing at this rapid rate, the market will continue to attach a premium multiple.

Investment Risk

While the growth trajectory is strong, there are risks to consider. For instance, North America TPV growth slowed to 21% y/y in 2H24, and while I remain optimistic, further deceleration could suggest ADYEY is facing competitive pressures from peers like Stripe and PYPL. As ADYEY moves upmarket, it also brings in the risk of take rate compression as large merchants have more power to negotiate better pricing. My opinion is that the market is very sensitive to ADYEY’s growth performance, so any signs of weakness could trigger a major derating.

Conclusion

My positive view of ADYEY is because it is a high-growth, high-quality business that is executing exceptionally well and has a lot of room to continue growing. The Unified Commerce and Platforms segments are ADYEY's main growth engines, which I expect to continue experiencing solid adoption. As long as ADYEY sustains its >20% y/y growth trajectory, I believe the market will continue to reward it with a premium multiple.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Caleres: A Surprising And Interesting Move For Stuart Weitzman
data.description:
Shares of Caleres, Inc. (NYSE:CAL) saw a relief move higher in February as the company announced a surprising deal after quite a tough period after the company had announced multiple guidance cuts in a row.

A dirt cheap valuation and modest leverage, in combination with a surprising and somewhat interesting deal, make me intrigued regarding the prospects of the pro forma business going forward, as I look forward to learning more about the impact of the deal in the coming months.

A Shoe Business

Caleres, founded in 1878, describes itself as a merchant-driven shoe organization with powerful brand assets, claiming expertise in sourcing, logistics, marketing analytics and technology.

A unique and synergistic structure, passionate and ambitious teams support strong brand names like Vionic, Allen Edmonds, Franco Sarto, Vince, LifeStride and Blowfish Malibu, to name a few.

In recent years the company has been on a stable footing (no pun intended) with sales having ranged between $2.8 and $3 billion in the period 2021-2023, as adjusted operating margins around 7% of sales allowed for earnings between $4.00 and $4.50 per share.

Zooming into the 2023 results, Caleres reported a 5% fall in annual sales to $2.82 billion as operating profits were down nearly a tenth to $194 million. GAAP earnings were down $10 million to $171 million, with GAAP earnings reported at $4.80 per share. These earnings were positively impacted by a deferred tax valuation allowance adjustment, with adjusted earnings reported at $4.18 per share.

These results were reported across two segments. The largest of these is a $1.61 billion Famous Footwear segment. Revenues in this segment were down over 5%, as the company managed to post segment margins at 7% and change, based on a share count of 860 stores by year-end.

The Brand Portfolio is a bit smaller with $1.27 billion in sales, but with just 98 stores, these stores on average are far more productive, also visible given the fact that operating margins come in at 11% and change.

For 2024, the company guided for flattish sales, with revenues seen between flat and positive 2%, and adjusted earnings seen up modestly to $4.30-$4.60 per share.

The Valuation Game

The 35 million shares of Caleres traded around the $40 mark early in 2024, grating equity in the business to a $1.4 billion valuation. This excluded a $161 million net debt load at the time (excluding lease assets and liabilities), with leverage being reasonable, as a $40 stock traded at 9-10 times adjusted earnings multiple.

After shares peaked at $45 in September of last year, shares have gradually come down to current levels of just $17. This was driven by three sequential cuts in the full-year outlook since September, with tough market conditions resulting in the lowered guidance. This calls for 2024 sales to be down 3.0-3.5%, but moreover, adjusted earnings are now seen at a midpoint of $3.25 per share.

Down to $17 per share, the 34 million shares grant equity of the business a mere $578 million valuation. This excludes a $204 million net debt load (which excludes lease liabilities and assets) for a $780 million enterprise valuation. With revenues seen close to $2.7 billion, the business trades at just a quarter of sales, with equity trading at just 5 times earnings here. With trailing adjusted EBITDA trending at $237 million, leverage ratios come in below 1 times, all looking quite reasonable.

A Big Deal

Amidst the difficulties of the own business, Caleres announced a surprising deal in February. The timing was of interest given the challenges faced by the business, but it was also the asset and the seller which surprised me.

Caleres announced a $105 million cash deal to acquire Stuart Weitzman from Tapestry, Inc. (TPR). At first sight, the deal looks like a bargain, as Tapestry bought the business nearly a decade ago at a price tag of over half a billion.

In its annual report, Tapestry announced a $241 million revenue contribution from Stuart Weitzman, although revenues were down substantially and the unit reported $21 million in segment losses.

Pro forma, the deal will boost sales by nearly 10%, yet Stuart Weitzman is posting losses. On the other hand, it might be of better allure, once and if the business is turned around. This ignites real potential for Caleres, but it will result in leverage increasing in absolute terms, but certainly in relative terms given the incurred losses which pressure near-term EBITDA while debt levels will go up.

Shares of Caleres moved up 5% in response to the deal, but this is just a $30 million move in reaction to a $105 million deal. Frankly, the near-term economics do not look too compelling, but the potential is there, but for that, one has to believe in management's ability to deliver on turning the brand around.

What Now?

Given the discussion above, I have mixed feelings about this deal. I see the near-term uncertainty, but also the long-term potential. For that, I look forward to more clues about the deal and the pro forma impact, due to be provided in March when the annual results are formally released.

For now, I'm appealed to the dirt cheap valuation and the intriguing deal, but not yet willing to commit as the business has seen a decade-long period of stagnation. However, I look forward to updating the thesis in March as more becomes known.

data.title: Alibaba Earnings Make It Official; Why I'm Buying China
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Alibaba (NYSE:BABA) just reported Q4 earnings, crushing expectations and soaring over 7% after the announcement. The company has seen encouraging dynamics in profitability and growth in Cloud, and also seems to be capturing upside from AI.

The outlook for BABA seems quite positive, and this comes at a time when it looks like the broader Chinese market may be on its way to recovery.

If China is once again investable, which I make a case here that it is, then BABA is one of the best stocks to gain exposure.

Based on past valuation metrics, the stock could still double from here.

BABA Q3 Earnings

Alibaba just reported a solid Q3 earnings bear, and the stock is up on the news.

Alibaba’s revenues came in at $38.4 billion, an 8% YoY increase. Taobao and Tmall increased 9%, Cloud revenues 13% and Digital Commerce leapfrogged 32%.

On the profitability side, we saw the company’s margins boosted through operational efficiency.

Adjusted EBITDA increased by 4% to $7.5 billion and net income increased 333% to $6.4 billion.

Alibaba also repurchased 119 million shares for over $1.3 billion and issued $55 billion in senior notes. The company also sold its stake in Sun Art and Intimate, worth a total combined of $2.6 billion

What’s Next For BABA?

After stellar earnings, Alibaba has attracted investors once more. The company is performing well and also has a bright outlook.

Like many of its tech peers in America, BABA is also focusing on AI in order to drive growth and profitability.

Cloud holds a lot of potential, and the company believes its AI products will sustain triple-digit.

On the e-commerce side, BABA is looking to improve customer loyalty through the expansion of its 88VIP benefits and is also expanding into other markets, recently South Korea, through a strategic JV.

On the financial side, it seems that the company is committed to financial discipline, focusing on its core business through divestitures and giving back to investors through continued share repurchases.

BABA has given investors a lot to be happy about after earnings, but let’s not forget that the stock has been rallying for the last month, up over 60% since recent lows.

And this is a trend we have observed in a more general sense.

Is China Investable Again?

The reception of these latest BABA earnings shows a clear change in sentiment in China, and this is also supported by market technicals.

The chart above shows the market breadth for the Hang Seng Index. As we can see, whenever this breaks above 60, we see a rally in Chinese stocks. These have been short in the past few instances, but this time could signal the beginning of a longer bull market.

The MSCI China ETF has also broken out of a falling wedge and is showing very strong momentum.

Challenges remain for 2025, and President Xi Jinping recently hosted a summit with business leaders in order to address some of the issues plaguing the Chinese economy.

But could the worst now be priced in?

How High Could BABA Go?

For a long time, BABA has faced two challenges.

Firstly, in the last few quarters, investors have doubted the company’s profitability and ability to pay back investors.

Secondly, BABA has suffered from the Chinese discount, as these stocks always trade at a discount compared to US stocks.

However, both these dark clouds are slowly lifting, and we could soon see BABA trade back near previous levels.

According to Gurufocus, BABA’s media PEG has been around 1.15. It now trades at 0.79 forward PE. If this ratio was closer to 1.15, then we'd see 40% upside from here.

This makes sense since it's still well below the sector median of 1.45. BABA would still trade at a discount, just not such a high discount.

Technical Analysis

40% upside from here would get us well into a key resistance level above, which sits around the $170-$190 region.

This level also lines up well with the 1.618 ext of the three-point fib extension from the recent high and low.

We’ve now broken above that January high, and we don’t have much resistance until we reach that zone above.

Risks

Nonetheless, there's still substantial risk when it comes to investing in BABA, perhaps now more than ever.

The new administration has made it very clear that it wants to impose global tariffs. So far, the market has digested these well. A lot of people seem to believe that tariffs are simply being used as bargaining chips, but things could still very much escalate from here.

Tariffs would hurt both China and the US in terms of GDP, which would impact BABA, and they could of course dampen investor sentiment, leading to an even larger discount on Chinese stocks

Final Thoughts

These latest earnings and the investor reaction have made it clear that investors are once again comfortable with Chinese stocks. BABA is perhaps the best way to gain exposure, with a strong business and bright future outlook.
